query: clinical manifestations of Asherman syndrome including menstrual pattern changes

retrieved_docs.title: Asherman Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with Asherman syndrome may have light or absent menstrual periods (amenorrhea). Still, some patients have normal periods based on the surface area of the cavity that is affected. Others have no periods but have severe dysmenorrhea (pain with menstruation) that may occur around the time of the anticipated menstrual period. The pain may indicate that the endometrial build-up occurs during the cycle, but that menstrual flow is obstructed due to adhesions near or within the cervix. These adhesions can present as recurrent miscarriage and/or infertility due to deficient endometrium and its poor vascularisation.

retrieved_docs.title: Asherman Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The chances of conceiving and delivery after surgery are lower in patients with moderate to severe disease but may improve after surgery if the cavity can be reconstructed and menses recur. Although a normal-appearing uterine cavity may be seen after repeated surgeries, rebuilding a normal endometrial lining may lag for some time after surgery or may not reoccur.

retrieved_docs.title: Asherman Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Outline the etiology of Asherman syndrome. Summarize the American Fertility Society classification of the severity of Asherman syndrome. Review the treatment considerations for patients with Asherman syndrome. Explain the importance of collaboration and communication among the interprofessional team to routinely monitor the endometrium for evidence of endometrial cancer which will improve outcomes in those with Asherman syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Asherman Syndrome -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: The American Fertility Society classifies the severity of the disease in three stages as follow: Mild disease: few filmy adhesions involving less than a third of the uterine cavity with normal menses or hypomenorrhea. Moderate disease: filmy and dense adhesions, the involvement of one-third to two-thirds of the cavity, and hypomenorrhea. Severe Disease: dense adhesions involving more than two-thirds of the cavity with amenorrhea.

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Female Pelvic Pathology Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation  -- Clinical Significance -- Infertility and Amenorrhoea
retrieved_docs.content: Asherman syndrome: In Asherman syndrome, the uterine cavity has multiple synechiae with damaged endometrium and compromised vascularity. This leads to amenorrhea, failed implantation, and infertility. On ultrasound, an interrupted endometrium line is noted in the sagittal plane with punctate echogenic foci within the endometrium. Echogenic fibrotic septae with or without calcification are seen on ultrasound. Asherman syndrome is better appreciated in 3D transvaginal sonography, which gives a 3D representation of the uterine cavity, which is noted as irregular with intermittent narrowing. [69] [70]

retrieved_docs.title: Secondary Amenorrhea -- Etiology -- Endometrial Abnormalities and Outflow Tract Obstruction
retrieved_docs.content: Intrauterine adhesions (Asherman syndrome) : If the basal layer of the endometrium is destroyed, deep scarring develops, and the endometrium cannot proliferate despite normal hormone profiles, resulting in secondary amenorrhea. Intrauterine adhesions may be caused by vigorous curettage, often in the setting of an intrauterine infection, after a miscarriage, therapeutic abortion, or postpartum hemorrhage. Additionally, tuberculous endometritis, seen primarily in developing regions of the world, can lead to extensive intrauterine adhesions and secondary amenorrhea.

retrieved_docs.title: Asherman Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Asherman syndrome may go unrecognized in women who are not trying to conceive since they may not recognize or be concerned with the symptoms. These women may have hypomenorrhea. Therefore, Asherman syndrome may be underdiagnosed because it is usually undetectable by routine examinations or diagnostic procedures such as an ultrasound scan. It may occur in up to 13% of women undergoing a termination of pregnancy during the first trimester, and 30% in women undergoing a dilation and curettage (D and C) after a late spontaneous abortion. Women with placental abnormalities (e.g., placenta increta) may have a higher risk of developing Asherman syndrome as the placenta adheres to deeper layers within the uterus and becomes more difficult to remove. [4] [6] The incidence may be as high as 23.4% in patients undergoing procedures two to four weeks after the initial procedure for a vaginal delivery or missed abortion. The risk increases for patients undergoing repeated procedures for bleeding or repeated elective termination of pregnancies.

retrieved_docs.title: Secondary Amenorrhea -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Asherman syndrome is treated with hysteroscopic lysis of adhesions.

retrieved_docs.title: Asherman Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The failure to induce menses despite stimulating the endometrium with an estrogen-progesterone challenge, in a patient with secondary amenorrhea, with normal hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian functions and hormone levels, points towards the diagnosis of Asherman's syndrome. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Asherman Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: It is found in 1.5% of women evaluated with a hysterosalpingogram (HSG) for infertility, between 5 and 39% of women with recurrent miscarriage. Asherman’s Syndrome may occur in 31% of women after the initial hysteroscopic resection of leiomyoma, and up to 46% after the second hysteroscopic resection.

retrieved_docs.title: Secondary Amenorrhea -- Evaluation -- Eugonadotropic findings
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to identifying polycystic appearing ovaries, the pelvic ultrasound may identify other ovarian abnormalities (eg, neoplasm, atrophy) and provide information about the uterus and cervix. For example, a thin endometrial lining suggests a hypoestrogenic state, while a thickened endometrial lining suggests the presence of estrogen and chronic anovulation. Hematometra (menstrual blood trapped within the uterus) identifies an outflow tract obstruction (eg, cervical stenosis). Intrauterine adhesions are typically not able to be identified on a regular ultrasound, though there may be some evidence of deep endometrial scarring at the myometrial junction.

retrieved_docs.title: Secondary Amenorrhea -- Etiology -- Endometrial Abnormalities and Outflow Tract Obstruction
retrieved_docs.content: Iatrogenic endometrial suppression : Progestins found in hormonal contraceptives are synthetic and result in endometrial atrophy. With prolonged use, the endometrium may become chronically suppressed, resulting in a lack of bleeding even during a scheduled hormone-free interval.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Menstrual Cycle -- Pathophysiology -- Abnormal Uterine Bleeding
retrieved_docs.content: Nonstructural causes of AUB include: C oagulopathy. O vulatory dysfunction, which includes endocrine and metabolic disorders affecting the HPO axis, such as PCOS, thyroid disorders, and elevated levels of prolactin, androgens, or cortisol. E ndometrial dysfunction, which includes conditions such as Asherman syndrome (destruction of the basal layer of the endometrium), inflammation (endometritis), and other issues that disrupt typical endometrial hemostasis. I atrogenic factors. N ot otherwise classified.

retrieved_docs.title: Asherman Syndrome -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: It has been inferred that Asherman’s syndrome has a constitutional component, explaining why it occurs only in a fraction of the individuals undergoing endometrial instrumentation and also explaining its occurrence in individuals without any preceding endometrial trauma. Besides, there appears to be a vulnerable time in the postpartum period when the process of curettage is likely to incite maximal damage to the endometrium, especially the basalis layer, resulting in a higher rate of adhesion formation. This period includes the second, third, and fourth postpartum weeks. [5] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Uterus -- Pathophysiology -- Asherman Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: Asherman syndrome occurs when collagen bands replace normal uterine tissue inside the uterus and/or cervix, causing intrauterine adhesions. [13] The basal layer of the endometrium undergoes fibrosis and cannot regenerate the functional layer. Without a functional layer, the endometrium does not respond to hormonal stimulation, and amenorrhea occurs. In severe cases, infertility can occur. Asherman syndrome is typically caused by uterine instrumentation, such as during a dilation and curettage (D&C).

retrieved_docs.title: Asherman Syndrome -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Although the risk of endometrial cancer in women with Asherman syndrome may be lower than the general population, women with Asherman may develop endometrial cancer before or after menopause. Since the signs and symptoms often found in these women (early or abnormal bleeding, thickened endometrium) may be missed due to the scarring or cervical obstruction; therefore, these women may benefit from a routine sonographic evaluation of the endometrium to monitor changes. [2] [14] Asherman's syndrome can result in obstetric complications that include preterm labor, Low birth weight(LBW), and placental complications that include retained placenta and placenta accreta. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 5 -- Community 2938
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and adenomyosis (انتباذ بطاني رحمي) are both gynecologic conditions for which epidemiological information is available, highlighting the importance of population‑level data in understanding their prevalence and risk factors. Epidemiology provides insight into how common each disorder is and which groups are most affected. Moreover, adenomyosis is directly related to female infertility, indicating that the presence of adenomyotic tissue within the uterine wall can contribute to difficulties conceiving. Recognizing the epidemiological patterns of both conditions and their interrelationship aids clinicians in identifying at‑risk patients, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing management strategies aimed at improving reproductive outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 537
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is linked hierarchically to both اضطراب الحمل (pregnancy disorder) and اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder) as parent categories, indicating that disorders of the female reproductive system can be considered subtypes of broader pregnancy‑related and uterine conditions. Within this overarching category, specific diseases such as cancer of the uterus and endometriosis are included. Endometriosis is further classified as a type of اضطراب الأعضاء التناسلية لدى الإناث (female genital organ disorder) and also as a form of اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder), reflecting its position both within the general female genital organ disorder hierarchy and the more specific uterine disorder group. The hierarchical structure therefore connects pregnancy disorders, uterine disorders, and female genital organ disorders, with Female Reproductive System Disorder, uterine cancer, and endometriosis positioned as specific manifestations within this network. This integrated view aids clinicians in understanding how various female reproductive conditions relate to each other and to broader reproductive health categories, supporting more comprehensive diagnosis and management.

Vaginal bleeding (نزيف مهبلي) is identified as a clinical manifestation that is associated with high‑risk patient status, highlighting the need for careful evaluation and management in patients presenting with this symptom. Together, these entities illustrate how various gynecologic and obstetric conditions are organized within standardized terminologies, linked to common risk factors such as high‑risk status and poor reproductive history, and connected to specific clinical interventions or classifications that guide patient assessment and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) is closely related to endometrial implantation (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). Abnormalities in the process of endometrial implantation can impair the uterine lining’s ability to support embryo attachment, thereby contributing to infertility in women. Recognizing the connection between these two conditions is important for clinicians when evaluating reproductive health and planning appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 730
retrieved_docs.content: Infertility in women of cervical origin is identified as a specific disorder of the cervix (اضطراب في عنق الرحم) and represents a subtype of female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث). Female infertility, in turn, is closely linked to intrauterine adhesions (انتباذ بطاني رحمي) and may arise as a consequence of inflammation of the uterine lining (الْتِهابُ بِطانَةِ الرَّحِمِ). These interconnections indicate that cervical pathology, uterine lining inflammation, and intrauterine adhesions can all contribute to the development of female infertility, underscoring the importance of evaluating cervical health, endometrial status, and the presence of adhesions when assessing and managing infertility in women.

Both the broader category other complications of pregnancy and the specific disorder are anatomically associated with the ženský reprodukční systém (female reproductive system). This reflects that disorders affecting the female reproductive system are considered part of the spectrum of pregnancy‑related complications.
